national_holiday.intro: Chile's Fiestas Patrias on 18 September celebrate the 1810 first governing junta and the country's independence.
national_holiday.history: On 18 September 1810 Chile formed its first governing junta, beginning the process toward independence from Spain, fully achieved in 1818. The 18th and 19th of September together form the ‚Dieciocho‘, Chile’s main national holiday.
national_holiday.traditions: Chileans flock to ‚ramadas‘ and ‚fondas‘ — festive pavilions — to dance the cueca, the national dance, eat empanadas and asado, and drink chicha. Flying the flag is mandatory, and celebrations span several days.
funFacts: The holiday is affectionately called 'el Dieciocho' (the 18th).
The cueca is Chile’s national dance, performed at fondas.
Flying the Chilean flag is legally required on this day.

navy_day.intro: Navy Day on 21 May honours Chile's naval heroes and the 1879 Battle of Iquique.
navy_day.history: On 21 May 1879, during the War of the Pacific, captain Arturo Prat and his crew died after their ship the Esmeralda was sunk by a Peruvian ironclad at Iquique. Their courage made Prat a national hero, and the date became a day of naval commemoration.
navy_day.traditions: The President traditionally gives the annual state-of-the-nation address on this day. Naval parades and ceremonies are held in port cities, especially Valparaíso and Iquique, honouring Arturo Prat and the fallen sailors.
funFacts: It honours captain Arturo Prat, who died at Iquique in 1879.
Chile lost the battle but gained a national hero.
The President’s annual address traditionally falls on this day.
